Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd try to set aside time on weekends to catch up on readings, but it rarely went according to plan. I have about 29 hours a week dedicated to studying across all my courses, but I'd often find myself just staring at the screen feeling overwhelmed. I prefer reading and writing things out, so I'd print materials when I could and work through them slowly, but motivation was hard to come by.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines. I'd have stretches where I barely touched anything, then suddenly I'd be cramming because something was due. Being 26 and juggling life stuff means I couldn't always stick to a perfect schedule. Deadlines were really the only thing that forced any consistency, and even then I'd sometimes miss the mark.
Q3: Usually it was because I remembered an assignment was coming up or I got a notification about something. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, which happened more than I'd like to admit with my attendance being what it is. It wasn't really a habit—more like damage control when I realized I'd been away too long.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were maybe a few weeks early on where I tried to make it a daily thing, but it never stuck. Life would get in the way, I'd feel sad or unmotivated, and then days would pass without me logging in. The LMS never became second nature to me like it seems to for other students.
Q5: The reading materials and written lecture notes were most useful because that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs and go through them at my own pace, highlighting and taking notes. Discussion boards were occasionally helpful when other students explained things in simpler terms. Video content was okay but I found myself zoning out a lot.
Q6: I mostly ignored the optional supplementary videos and extra readings. When you're already struggling to keep up with the required stuff, optional materials feel like a luxury you can't afford. I also didn't engage much with peer interactions beyond what was necessary—I just didn't have the energy for it most of the time.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see how it would help my grade, I'd make the effort. Anything that felt abstract or disconnected from assessments was hard to justify when I was already stretched thin and feeling low.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore, honestly. Required ones had consequences attached, so I'd force myself through them even when I didn't want to. Optional stuff just got pushed to a 'maybe later' pile that I never actually returned to. The pressure of requirements was the only thing that got me moving most days.
Q9: Yes, a lot of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download the readings and PDFs, print them out when possible, and work through them with a pen and notebook. I also used external resources like YouTube videos or other websites when the course materials didn't click. The LMS was more of a starting point than where the real learning happened.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours going through printed notes or re-reading materials I'd saved to my computer. I'd be genuinely trying to understand concepts, writing summaries, making connections—but none of that showed up anywhere. My best study sessions were often when I was completely disconnected from the internet, just me and my notes.
Q11: Definitely. I'd access a reading or resource once, download it, and then never go back to the LMS version. I preferred having things saved locally so I could study without needing internet or dealing with the platform. Some materials I probably reviewed five or six times offline after just one download.
Q12: There were several rough patches, especially mid-semester. I was dealing with some personal sadness that made everything feel pointless. Getting out of bed was hard enough, let alone engaging with coursework. My resilience isn't great, so when things got tough, I'd retreat rather than push through. The CBT sessions helped somewhat, but the struggle was real.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was stressed or exhausted, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Having ten online courses meant constant competing demands, and sometimes I'd just shut down completely. Confusion about what was expected also made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face how behind I was.
Q14: More times than I can count. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through readings without actually absorbing anything. My eyes were on the screen but my brain had checked out. It was like going through the motions without any real connection to the material.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially bigger assignments that felt overwhelming. I knew they were important but the thought of starting made me anxious, so I'd avoid them until the last possible moment. It's a terrible cycle—the more I avoided, the more stressed I got, which made me avoid even more. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would become next week.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me engage with the LMS consistently. I'd have bursts of activity in the days leading up to due dates, trying to cram everything in. Without deadlines, I'd drift for weeks. It's not healthy, but that external pressure was often the only thing that could cut through my fog.
Q17: Feedback had mixed effects on me. Good feedback gave me a temporary boost, but it faded quickly. Negative or critical feedback sometimes made me want to disengage even more because it confirmed my fears that I wasn't good enough. I tried to use feedback constructively, but emotionally it was hard not to take it personally.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so many modules and expectations. When things were clearly laid out with specific deadlines, I could manage somewhat. But when there was flexibility or self-pacing expected, I'd fall behind because I lack the internal drive to stay on track. I needed more external structure than was provided.
Q19: Having materials available for download made engagement easier since I could work offline. But the sheer volume of content across my courses made it harder—I never felt like I could catch up. Discussion requirements felt forced and added stress without adding much value for me personally. Clearer, simpler pathways would have helped.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and keeps checking things obsessively without actually completing work. Or they might be confused about what's expected and keep looking for answers without finding them. Sometimes high activity is actually a sign of struggle, not engagement—like spinning your wheels without moving forward.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. My LMS activity was low, but I did pass the course because a lot of my real studying happened offline. If you just looked at my login frequency, you'd think I wasn't trying at all. But I was working through materials in my own way, just not in ways the system could track.
Q22: Course logs can show when you accessed things and for how long, which gives a surface-level picture. But they completely miss the quality of engagement—whether someone was actually thinking or just clicking through. They also miss offline study, emotional state, and all the invisible struggles that affect how we learn.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it. It's not just completing tasks but understanding why they matter. For me, true engagement has been rare this semester—mostly I've been surviving rather than thriving. When I am engaged, I'm curious and want to dig deeper, but that feeling has been hard to access lately.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally throughout the course. It wouldn't capture the sadness that made simple tasks feel impossible, or the effort it took just to submit anything at all. It also wouldn't show the offline studying I did, or how the CBT sessions were helping me slowly work through my issues. My data looks like disengagement, but it's really more complicated than that.
